text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — POST-TREATMENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology]
VISIT TYPE: Post-Chemoradiation Follow-up (Week 4)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up 4 weeks after completion of definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT for HPV-positive left base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma (T2N1M0, Stage III, AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging system).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoking East Asian woman with HPV-16-positive, p16-positive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with left level IIA nodal involvement who was treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation. She received intensity-modulated radiation therapy to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary site and gross nodal disease, with elective bilateral nodal coverage, completed over 7 weeks. She received 2 of 3 planned cycles of high-dose cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks; the third cycle was held due to creatinine elevation to 1.32 mg/dL (from baseline 0.78 mg/dL) during week 6 of treatment. Cumulative cisplatin administered: approximately 340 mg/m2. The final fraction of radiation was delivered approximately 4 weeks ago. She presents today for her first formal post-treatment evaluation.

The patient reports her course since completing treatment has been slow but steady improvement. She describes the first two weeks after finishing radiation as the worst — worsening mucositis, continued severe odynophagia, and fatigue. She is now eating soft foods and liquid supplement shakes, managing approximately 800-1000 kcal orally per day, and continues to rely on her PEG tube (placed during week 5 of chemoradiation) for the remainder of her caloric needs. She has regained a modest amount of swallowing confidence over the past week. She is running Jevity 1.5 at 55 mL/hour overnight plus a bolus feed in the afternoon.

Oral pain has improved substantially compared to end-of-treatment. She no longer requires around-the-clock oxycodone; she is taking oxycodone 5 mg as needed, approximately 1-2 times per day, primarily in the morning before attempting oral intake. Acetaminophen around-the-clock for background analgesia.

She reports a persistent altered taste — foods taste "metallic" and she has lost interest in eating, though she appreciates the gradual improvement. Significant dry mouth is present; she keeps a water bottle at all times and uses a saliva spray. She denies any new throat pain, neck swelling, fevers, or chills. She denies ear pain. Mild persistent fatigue, improved compared to end-of-treatment. No cough, no shortness of breath.

Her neck skin has healed from the moist desquamation that was present at treatment completion; the bilateral anterior neck skin now shows dry desquamation with some residual erythema.

She has not yet returned to work. Her husband has been providing primary caregiver support at home. Mood and affect are guarded but she expresses optimism.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive for: odynophagia (improving, mild-moderate), dysgeusia, xerostomia, fatigue, mild tingling in bilateral fingertips (present since approximately week 5 of treatment).
Negative for: dysphagia to liquids, new neck mass or swelling, fevers, chills, unintentional weight loss beyond documented treatment-related weight loss, hemoptysis, hematemesis, otalgia, nasal obstruction, epistaxis, diplopia, facial numbness, dyspnea, chest pain, lower extremity edema.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
Weight: 55.0 kg (pre-treatment baseline 62 kg; 7 kg total loss, 11.3% body weight loss).
General: Alert, oriented, appears fatigued but in no acute distress. Conversational voice quality slightly muffled but intelligible.
Head/Face: No facial asymmetry. No trismus — mouth opens to approximately 4 cm interincisal distance.
Oral cavity: Residual erythema of the oral mucosa and tongue. Grade 1 mucositis — generalized erythema without ulceration or pseudomembrane formation. No active bleeding. Tongue midline. No visible lesion at the oral tongue. Mild white coating on dorsal tongue consistent with candidal colonization; will send oral rinse if clinical candidiasis suspected.
Oropharynx: Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in clinic. Left base of tongue mucosa appears diffusely thickened and erythematous consistent with post-radiation change; no discrete mass or exophytic lesion identified. Right base of tongue and lingual tonsils unremarkable. Epiglottis mildly edematous at the free margin, no supraglottic mass. True vocal cords visualize well; mobile bilaterally without fixation. No pooling of secretions noted on flexible laryngoscopy. Left vallecula appears patent.
Neck: Left level IIA region — no palpable lymphadenopathy. No firmness or fullness in the left neck. No palpable nodes bilaterally. Bilateral anterior neck skin with residual dry desquamation and hyperpigmentation; erythema substantially faded compared to end-of-treatment. Mild soft tissue induration of the anterior neck bilaterally, consistent with early post-radiation fibrosis. No skin breakdown or wound.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Mild symmetric decreased sensation to light touch over the distal fingertips bilaterally; consistent with grade 1 peripheral neuropathy. No gross motor deficits.
Cardiopulmonary: Regular rate and rhythm; lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Extremities: PEG tube site at left mid-abdomen, intact, no erythema or surrounding induration. Tube secured appropriately.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
Complete metabolic panel:
  - Creatinine: 0.85 mg/dL (baseline 0.78; improved from peak 1.32 mg/dL during treatment)
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - Na 139, K 3.8, CO2 26 — within normal limits
  - Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L — normal
  - Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (down from 4.1 g/dL at baseline; consistent with nutritional depletion)
CBC:
  - WBC 5.2 x 10^9/L, ANC adequate
  - Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia, likely treatment-related)
  - Platelets 218 x 10^9/L
Magnesium: 1.6 mEq/L (low-normal; hypomagnesemia was present during treatment requiring IV repletion; will continue oral magnesium supplementation)
Thyroid function: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (normal; post-radiation hypothyroidism screening initiated, will follow serially)

---

IMAGING:
Pre-existing imaging reviewed in preparation for this visit:
  - Pre-treatment staging PET-CT: hypermetabolic left BOT primary (SUVmax 12.4), single left level IIA nodal metastasis (SUVmax 9.8); no distant disease.
  - Pre-treatment MRI neck: 2.2 x 1.9 cm left BOT mass, left level IIA node 2.1 cm with central necrosis, no extranodal extension radiographically.
Post-treatment restaging imaging not yet performed (planned at approximately 12 weeks post-treatment completion per institutional protocol — to allow adequate time for post-treatment inflammation to resolve and avoid false-positive findings). Today's clinical evaluation and flexible laryngoscopy are reassuring for response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0) — 4 weeks post-completion of definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT.
   - Clinical examination and flexible nasopharyngoscopy today are reassuring; no visible or palpable residual disease. The post-radiation mucosal thickening noted endoscopically is expected and does not suggest persistent tumor.
   - Will proceed with formal post-treatment restaging PET-CT at approximately 12 weeks post-RT completion (approximately 8 weeks from today). Formal restaging CT neck with contrast will also be obtained at that time point per our standard surveillance template. Routine clinical examination and flexible laryngoscopy at each visit in the interim.
   - Patient and family counseled on the highly favorable prognosis of HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC treated with definitive CRT and achieving clinical complete response. Counseled that formal imaging is deferred to allow inflammatory changes to settle.

2. Post-treatment mucositis/odynophagia — improving, now grade 1.
   - Continue magic mouthwash, sodium bicarb rinses, good oral hygiene.
   - Continue oxycodone 5 mg PRN for breakthrough pain; encourage weaning as tolerated. Continue acetaminophen 650 mg q6h ATC.
   - Oral candidal colonization noted on exam; prescribing oral nystatin suspension 5 mL swish-and-swallow QID x 14 days empirically given mucosal changes and ongoing steroid rinse use.

3. Nutritional status/PEG tube — patient dependent on enteral feeds for majority of caloric intake.
   - Current weight 55 kg; albumin 3.4 g/dL. Goal is gradual improvement in oral intake over the coming weeks.
   - Continue current Jevity 1.5 regimen (overnight continuous + afternoon bolus, ~1800 kcal/day) with adjustments as oral intake increases.
   - Referral placed to speech-language pathology for post-treatment swallowing evaluation and dysphagia therapy to facilitate PEG tube weaning. Target decannulation within 8-12 weeks if oral intake adequately progresses.
   - Continue oral magnesium oxide 400 mg BID for ongoing hypomagnesemia. Recheck magnesium in 3-4 weeks.

4. Xerostomia — grade 1-2, persistent.
   - Continue saliva substitutes (Biotene products), frequent water sips, good oral hygiene.
   - No role for pilocarpine at this stage but can revisit if xerostomia remains significant at the 3-month visit.

5. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral fingertips) — attributed to cisplatin.
   - No treatment indicated at this grade. Will monitor at each visit. If worsens, consider gabapentin or duloxetine.
   - Cumulative cisplatin 340 mg/m2 total — within the range for mild neuropathy; risk of progression after treatment completion is low but real.

6. Post-radiation hypothyroidism screening — TSH 2.8 mIU/L today (normal).
   - Will recheck TSH in 3 months. Radiation to the neck puts her at risk for hypothyroidism over 12-24 months post-treatment; she should be aware of symptoms (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain).

7. Nephrotoxicity — creatinine recovered to 0.85 mg/dL (near baseline). No further cisplatin anticipated.

8. Return to follow-up: 6 weeks (to assess ongoing recovery from acute treatment toxicities, monitor swallowing function, and advance PEG tube weaning plan). Will then see at 12 weeks post-RT for formal restaging imaging review.

Time spent: 45 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed — Head and Neck Medical Oncology Attending]
